name: Romantic Comedy
description: Romance structured around humor and social friction, typically moving from mismatch and misunderstanding toward commitment or reconciliation. (This graph's constraint definitions are intentionally more detailed than the genre average to capture the nuanced relationship dynamics central to Romantic Comedy.)

nodes.label: Romantic Comedy Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised a love story told through humor, wit, and social friction â€” two people who are wrong for each other (or think they are) will navigate comic obstacles and arrive at romantic union or reconciliation. Both the love story and the comedy are contractual: neither can be dropped without betraying the genre.
entry_conditions: Story is positioned as romantic comedy
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that guarantee both a romantic arc and a comic arc running simultaneously
typical_variants: Screwball romance
Workplace rom-com
Friends-to-lovers
Fake relationship / marriage of convenience
failure_modes: Delivers romance without consistent humor â€” becomes melodrama
Delivers humor without genuine romantic feeling â€” becomes pure comedy
Neither romance nor comedy land with sufficient force
signals_in_text: Opening establishes both a romantic possibility and a comic obstacle to that possibility
Tone is warm and playful even when characters are in conflict
Audience roots for the couple despite (or because of) their friction

nodes.label: Romance and Humor Must Be Co-Equal Engines
nodes.definition: Neither romance nor comedy may dominate exclusively. The story fails if it becomes so romantic that humor evaporates, or so comedic that genuine feeling is sacrificed. Every major scene should serve both the love story and the comic texture â€” an argument is both funny and emotionally revealing, a misunderstanding both absurd and genuinely threatening to the relationship.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
exit_conditions: Both romantic longing and comic friction are present and active in the majority of scenes
typical_variants: Verbal sparring with undeniable chemistry
Comic situation that forces emotional honesty
Humorous misunderstanding that also deepens genuine stakes
Playful banter that doubles as flirtation
failure_modes: Story drifts into straight romance â€” humor becomes occasional relief rather than engine
Story drifts into pure comedy â€” romantic feeling is treated ironically and never believed
Comedy undercuts genuine emotional moments before they can land
signals_in_text: Scenes contain at least one laugh and at least one beat of genuine longing or emotional vulnerability
Characters' arguments reveal what they actually want from each other
Audience simultaneously finds moments funny and feels the romantic pull

nodes.label: Couple Must Be Mismatched or in Conflict Before Coming Together
nodes.definition: The central couple must begin in a state of mismatch, opposition, or conflict â€” they are from different worlds, hold opposing values, have incompatible goals, or actively dislike each other. This initial friction is the source of both the comedy and the romantic tension. Union is only meaningful if it overcomes genuine incompatibility.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
Central couple introduced
exit_conditions: Mismatch or conflict is clearly established before any romantic progress is made
typical_variants: Class or cultural difference
Professional rivalry or opposing goals
Established antipathy or bad first impression
Incompatible worldviews that generate sustained friction
Forced proximity despite mutual resistance
failure_modes: Couple is compatible from the start â€” obstacles are purely external rather than relational
Mismatch is superficial and resolved too quickly to generate sustained comedy or tension
Conflict is so serious that romantic union feels implausible
signals_in_text: Early scenes show the couple in opposition or mutual incomprehension
Leads explicitly or implicitly dismiss each other as a romantic possibility
Their differences generate both comic incongruity and genuine dramatic stakes

nodes.label: Social Friction Generates Both the Comedy and the Obstacles
nodes.definition: In romantic comedy, it is social friction â€” the web of friends, family, coworkers, rivals, and expectations â€” that creates both the humor and the impediments to love. The obstacles are not purely internal (characters' fears) or purely external (circumstance); they are socially generated. Other people complicate, witness, amplify, and ultimately validate the relationship.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
Social world around the couple established
exit_conditions: At least one major source of socially-generated friction shapes the comic and romantic arcs
typical_variants: Disapproving family or friends whose opinion matters
Rival suitors or ex-partners who complicate the field
Social expectations about appropriate partners or behavior
Public humiliation or exposure that raises comic and romantic stakes
Third parties who misread or meddle in the central relationship
failure_modes: Couple exists in social isolation â€” no one cares what they do
Friction is purely internal (therapy drama) without comic social dimension
Social world is present but inert â€” characters have no real social stakes
signals_in_text: Secondary characters have strong opinions about the central couple
Public or semi-public scenes where social performance is at stake
Misunderstandings travel through the social network and grow
Resolution requires the social world to witness or ratify the union

nodes.label: Resolution Must Deliver Both Romantic and Comic Satisfaction
nodes.definition: The ending must pay off both the romantic promise (the couple commits, reconciles, or acknowledges love) and the comic promise (the final obstacle is overcome in a way that is emotionally satisfying and at least somewhat funny or charming). A resolution that is purely romantic (tearful reunion, no comic energy) or purely comedic (laugh at the expense of genuine feeling) fails the genre contract.
entry_conditions: Comic and romantic arcs established
exit_conditions: Story ends with clear romantic union or commitment and at least one final beat that is warm, charming, or witty
typical_variants: Grand romantic gesture that is also slightly ridiculous and self-aware
Truth-telling scene that is both honest and comic in its delivery
Final reunion that releases both romantic and comic tension simultaneously
Epilogue showing couple in their new dynamic â€” still playful, clearly together
failure_modes: Resolution is purely earnest â€” every comic note is abandoned for sincerity
Resolution is purely comic â€” romance is undercut by irony or joke
Romantic union happens off-screen or is insufficiently dramatized
Comic final obstacle is resolved without romantic acknowledgment
signals_in_text: Climax involves both a declaration or gesture of love and a moment of levity
Final scenes show couple together in their natural comic dynamic
Audience leaves feeling both emotionally satisfied and charmed

nodes.label: Screwball Romance
nodes.definition: Fast-paced romantic comedy driven by rapid verbal sparring, battle-of-wills dynamics, and social-class friction between the leads. The couple's romantic attraction manifests primarily as conflict â€” they fight because they are drawn to each other, and the audience reads the chemistry beneath the combat. Resolution comes when both characters stop performing superiority and admit desire.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
Leads are both witty and evenly matched
Comic energy is primarily verbal and behavioral rather than situational
exit_conditions: Verbal sparring is the dominant comic engine and generates both laughs and romantic tension
typical_variants: Class-collision sparring (aristocrat vs. commoner)
Professional rivals forced to collaborate
Battle-of-the-sexes with evolving power dynamic
Chaotic pursuit where one character disrupts the other's ordered life
failure_modes: Verbal exchanges are one-sided â€” only one character is funny or dominant
Sparring tips into genuine cruelty or contempt that poisons the romantic possibility
Pace slackens and the witty-combat energy dissipates
Chemistry never convinces â€” audience doesn't believe the attraction beneath the fighting
signals_in_text: Rapid-fire dialogue with overlapping or interrupted speech
Characters regularly outmaneuver each other in alternating victories
Physical comedy accompanies verbal sparring â€” pratfalls, chases, physical slapstick
Leads are clearly watching each other even as they pretend not to care

nodes.label: Workplace Rom-Com
nodes.definition: Romantic comedy set in a professional environment where workplace hierarchy, professional stakes, and the rules of appropriate behavior create the primary comic and romantic friction. The leads must navigate their attraction within a context that makes that attraction dangerous, inappropriate, or professionally compromising.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
Both leads share a professional context
Professional stakes complicate or prohibit the romance
exit_conditions: Professional setting actively generates the comic situations and the obstacles to romance
typical_variants: Boss-employee dynamic with power-imbalance comedy
Colleagues competing for the same promotion while falling for each other
Professional rivals in the same field whose work brings them into constant proximity
New hire disrupts established workplace dynamics and one senior employee's composure
failure_modes: Workplace is merely a backdrop â€” professional stakes don't shape the romance or comedy
Professional conflict resolves too early, leaving only a generic love story
Power imbalance is played for drama rather than comedy, poisoning the genre tone
Secondary coworkers are undifferentiated and fail to amplify comic stakes
signals_in_text: Professional achievements or failures are linked to the romantic arc
Scenes are set in meetings, offices, or work events where decorum is at stake
Other coworkers observe, comment on, or meddle in the central relationship
A professional decision must be made that directly conflicts with romantic desire

nodes.label: Friends-to-Lovers
nodes.definition: Romantic comedy in which the leads begin as established friends or familiar companions, and the comedy arises from their obliviousness to (or suppression of) romantic feelings that the audience can clearly see. The mismatch is internal: they are well-matched in every way except that one or both refuses to recognize the obvious. Resolution requires both acknowledging what has been building in plain sight.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
Leads have established friendship or long familiarity before romantic feelings surface
Comic irony arises from the gap between audience perception and character denial
exit_conditions: Friendship context generates sustained comedy from dramatic irony and near-misses
typical_variants: Long-term friends who confess simultaneously after years of mutual denial
One friend pines while the other pursues unsuitable partners â€” each new partner raises comic and dramatic stakes
External event or third party forces both to confront suppressed feelings
Friends who bet or make a pact about love that backfires on them both
failure_modes: Friendship is stated but not shown â€” audience doesn't see why they'd be friends
Dramatic irony is absent â€” audience knows no more than the characters
Transition from friendship to romance is abrupt and insufficiently earned
Comedy relies on repetitive near-miss rather than escalating comic situation
signals_in_text: Early scenes show genuine warmth and easy familiarity between the leads
Secondary characters remark on the obvious romantic potential the leads deny
Near-confessions are interrupted or deflected at key moments
Each unsuitable partner or rival raises the comic and emotional pressure

nodes.label: Fake Relationship / Marriage of Convenience
nodes.definition: Romantic comedy in which the leads enter a false or pragmatic romantic arrangement â€” pretending to date, agreeing to a marriage of convenience, or staging a relationship for social or professional purposes. Comedy arises from their attempts to perform romance while denying real feelings; the central irony is that the fake relationship reveals genuine compatibility and attraction the characters must eventually acknowledge.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
The leads establish a contractual or performative romantic arrangement for external reasons
Both initially understand the relationship to be artificial
exit_conditions: The fake arrangement is the primary source of comic situation and generates escalating romantic feelings that complicate the original contract
typical_variants: Fake dating to appease family at a holiday or wedding
Marriage of convenience for legal, financial, or professional benefit
Staged relationship to make an ex jealous
Pretend engagement to satisfy a deadline (inheritance, visa, job requirement)
Hired partner for a public event whose deception must be sustained across multiple social encounters
failure_modes: The external reason for the fake relationship is implausible or unearned
Characters fall for each other too quickly â€” the fake arrangement phase is too short
Conflict when the fake relationship is exposed is resolved without sufficient comic consequence
Real feelings are declared too easily â€” the denial phase is not mined for comic and romantic tension
signals_in_text: Characters explicitly negotiate the terms of their arrangement
They must perform romantic intimacy for an audience while privately maintaining their pretense
Small genuine moments of connection are immediately suppressed or rationalized
The truth threatens to surface at maximally inconvenient social moments

nodes.label: Setting Forces Repeated Interaction Between Leads
nodes.definition: The romantic comedy setting must make it impossible or very difficult for the leads to avoid each other. Forced proximity is a structural necessity: if the characters can simply walk away from the friction, the comedy deflates. The setting â€” shared workplace, same social circle, neighboring apartments, road trip, shared living space, recurring professional obligation â€” engineers repeated contact and escalating mutual knowledge.
entry_conditions: Subgenre pattern selected
Central couple established with initial mismatch or conflict
exit_conditions: Setting guarantees the leads will interact repeatedly whether they wish to or not
typical_variants: Shared professional space (office, set, firm)
Shared living arrangements (neighboring units, same house, same hotel)
Shared social network that produces recurring gatherings
Ongoing project or task that requires collaboration
Geographic or logistical constraint (small town, road trip, island, event venue)
failure_modes: Characters can easily avoid each other and the story must contrive reasons they don't
Proximity is established early but not maintained â€” the setting loses its forcing function
Forced proximity produces only tolerance rather than escalating comic friction and growing intimacy
signals_in_text: Leads encounter each other in multiple distinct contexts generated by the setting
Each encounter leaves them both more aware of the other, even if that awareness is expressed as irritation
The setting itself is commented on â€” characters acknowledge they keep running into each other

nodes.label: Social Audience Amplifies Comic Stakes
nodes.definition: Friends, family, coworkers, and rivals who surround the central couple serve as an active social audience that witnesses, interprets, amplifies, and meddles in the romance. Their presence raises the stakes: misunderstandings don't stay private, embarrassments become public, and the social world has strong opinions about whether the leads belong together. The social audience also provides comic relief, foils, and the external validation that the genre's resolution typically requires.
entry_conditions: Setting established
Secondary characters introduced with relationship to leads
exit_conditions: Secondary characters actively shape the comic and romantic arc, not merely populate the world
typical_variants: Best friend who sees the truth before the protagonist does and comments on it
Disapproving parent or sibling whose opinion the protagonist cannot dismiss
Well-meaning friend who gives terrible advice that worsens the comic situation
Rival who raises the stakes by threatening to take one lead's attention
Greek chorus of coworkers or neighbors who track the relationship's progress
failure_modes: Secondary characters are decorative â€” they observe but don't affect the story
No character in the social world perceives or comments on the central couple's dynamic
Social audience is present but uniformly supportive â€” no friction or competing perspective
Best friend or confidant only serves to receive exposition without comic effect
signals_in_text: Secondary characters discuss the central couple when they are absent
Leads modify their behavior based on who is watching
Misunderstandings are witnessed and misinterpreted by third parties who then act on that misreading
Resolution requires social world to witness the couple's commitment

nodes.label: Public vs. Private Behavior Diverges
nodes.definition: A reliable source of romantic comedy is the gap between how characters behave in public (performing indifference, composure, or antipathy) and how they behave in private (revealing vulnerability, longing, or genuine self). This divergence drives both comedy â€” the performance is funny in its transparency â€” and romance â€” the private moments are where genuine feeling surfaces. Settings that allow both public and private scenes exploit this gap effectively.
entry_conditions: Setting established with both social/public and private contexts
Leads have reason to maintain a public performance that differs from private feeling
exit_conditions: At least one major scene exploits the gap between characters' public performance and private reality
typical_variants: Character performs hostility in public and tenderness in private
Leads must pretend to dislike each other for professional or social reasons while privately drawn together
Private moments of accidental honesty that must be walked back publicly
One character witnesses the other's private behavior and cannot un-know what they've seen
failure_modes: Characters behave identically in public and private â€” no gap to exploit
Public performance is so convincing that audience forgets the private truth
Private moments are never shown â€” only public behavior exists, leaving romance underdeveloped
signals_in_text: Scene structure alternates between public social scenes and private or semi-private encounters
Characters' body language, tone, or word choice shifts when they move between public and private context
Overheard conversations or accidental witnesses create comic and romantic complications

nodes.label: Meet-Cute or Charged First Encounter Scene
nodes.definition: An early scene â€” often the first encounter between leads â€” that establishes the mismatch, generates the first major comic beat, and simultaneously signals the romantic possibility. The meet-cute is charged with both friction and potential: it is funny, slightly awkward or adversarial, and yet the audience perceives chemistry that the characters deny or misread. This scene sets the entire tonal and relational contract for the story.
entry_conditions: Story opening establishes world and leads
Neither character has yet acknowledged any romantic feeling for the other
exit_conditions: First encounter is complete; mismatch is established; audience has seen both the comic friction and the romantic potential; characters have a reason to interact again
typical_variants: Literal physical collision or accident that creates inconvenient intimacy
Adversarial professional first meeting where both are trying to prevail
Mistaken identity or misread context that creates an awkward false impression
Situation where one character accidentally witnesses the other in a private or unguarded moment
Forced collaboration on a shared problem neither wanted to share
failure_modes: First encounter is too smooth â€” no friction, no comic beat, no memorable incongruity
Characters are immediately and mutually attracted with no comedy of mismatch
Encounter is memorable to the audience but not to the characters â€” no lasting impression is made
Comic element overwhelms any romantic signal â€” audience sees only comedy, not chemistry
signals_in_text: Scene has a specific, memorable premise that distinguishes it from an ordinary introduction
Both leads leave the encounter having formed a strong (and usually wrong) impression of the other
Audience is given more information than the characters â€” sees potential they cannot yet see
Scene ends with an unresolved tension that makes the next encounter inevitable

nodes.label: Misunderstanding Escalation Scene (Comedy Peaks)
nodes.definition: A scene or sequence â€” typically in the middle act â€” where a misunderstanding, deception, or comic complication reaches its maximum intensity. This is where the comedy peaks: the situation is at its most absurd, the misreading of the relationship is at its most entrenched, and the gap between what is true and what the characters believe is at its widest. This scene is also the romantic nadir â€” the couple is furthest from each other just as the audience most wants them together.
entry_conditions: Central misunderstanding or comic complication established earlier
Leads have made some romantic progress that is now at risk
External comic situation has been escalating across multiple scenes
exit_conditions: Comic situation has reached its peak absurdity; romantic distance is at maximum; the trajectory toward resolution has begun or is clearly implied; audience is both laughing and feeling the romantic stakes acutely
typical_variants: Overheard partial conversation taken out of context that confirms the wrong impression
Fake relationship is nearly exposed at maximum social inconvenience
Third-party interference causes both leads to act on false information simultaneously
Comic lie or deception expands to require performance in a high-stakes public setting
Both characters independently reach wrong conclusions that move them further apart
failure_modes: Misunderstanding peaks too early and the story has nowhere to go
Comic escalation is separate from the romantic arc â€” funny but doesn't affect the relationship
Peak moment is visible only to the audience but doesn't shift the characters' actions or feelings
Scene relies on single big misunderstanding rather than layered accumulated comic pressure
signals_in_text: Maximum number of wrong assumptions are active simultaneously
Characters make decisions based on false information that will have lasting consequences
Audience feels the delicious agony of knowing what the characters don't
Scene ends with the couple at their greatest emotional distance â€” reunion seems genuinely difficult from here

nodes.label: Grand Romantic Gesture / Truth-Telling Scene
nodes.definition: The climactic scene in which the misunderstanding is dispelled, the truth is told, and one or both leads makes a grand romantic gesture â€” an act that requires genuine vulnerability and public commitment. This scene must pay off the entire comic and romantic arc: the gesture is both sincere and suffused with the personality and humor of the characters. It should be emotionally earned, not merely conventional.
entry_conditions: Comic misunderstanding or obstacle has been cleared or is in the process of clearing
At least one lead has fully recognized and accepted their feelings
The social audience is present or will learn of the outcome
exit_conditions: Truth is out; romantic commitment is acknowledged; both the romantic and comic arcs are resolved; the couple is united or reconciled with emotional and social specificity
typical_variants: Public declaration in front of the social audience (airport, party, workplace, family gathering)
Private truth-telling that requires the character to abandon their defensive posture entirely
Grand physical gesture that requires comic self-deprecation (the romantic is slightly ridiculous and knows it)
Characters finally speaking honestly after long comic evasion â€” the truth is funny precisely because it's obvious in retrospect
Gesture that directly references the meet-cute or a specific earlier moment â€” the arc completes with specific emotional resonance
failure_modes: Gesture is generic â€” could belong to any love story, with no connection to this couple's specific comic history
Truth-telling is too easy â€” character confesses without having to overcome anything
Grand gesture is played entirely for laughs with no genuine emotional vulnerability
Resolution happens in private with no social witness, depriving the scene of its full tonal charge
One character does all the work â€” the other merely receives without reciprocal act or revelation
signals_in_text: Scene directly inverts or completes an earlier scene from the story
Character must physically move toward the other person â€” the gesture requires effort and risk
Comic self-awareness coexists with genuine sincerity in the same moment
Secondary characters react â€” the social world confirms the resolution
Final exchange has the rhythm and wit of the couple's established dynamic â€” they are recognizably themselves, and better

nodes.label: Wit, Warmth, and Playful Energy
nodes.definition: Romantic comedy requires a tone that holds wit, warmth, and playful energy in balance. Wit without warmth becomes cold and cynical â€” the comedy punishes rather than celebrates. Warmth without wit becomes saccharine â€” the story feels naive rather than charming. Playful energy is the animating quality that makes the audience feel they are in good hands: the narrative knows it is playing a game and plays it with skill and affection.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
exit_conditions: Tone is sustained across the story such that even the most painful romantic moments retain playful energy, and even the funniest moments retain genuine warmth
typical_variants: Sharp and verbal (screwball tradition)
Warm and character-driven (friends-to-lovers)
Gently absurdist (situational comedy with heart)
Dry and self-aware (modern rom-com that acknowledges genre conventions)
failure_modes: Tone becomes mean-spirited â€” humor is at characters' lasting expense
Warmth collapses into sentimentality that undercuts the comedy
Playful energy disappears during the 'dark night of the soul' third act and never fully returns
Wit is verbal only â€” physical performance and situation are played straight, creating tonal imbalance
signals_in_text: Narrative voice or dialogue style maintains lightness even when describing painful emotional situations
Characters can be embarrassed or hurt and still be funny â€” their suffering is temporary and affectionately observed
The story's irony never extends to contempt for the couple or for romantic love itself
Secondary characters contribute to tone â€” the world around the leads is also warm and playful

nodes.label: Romance Without Chemistry, Comedy Without Heart
nodes.definition: The fundamental romantic comedy failure: a love story where the audience never believes in the attraction between the leads (romance without chemistry), or a comedy where the humor is so detached from genuine feeling that the romantic resolution feels unearned (comedy without heart). The two failures are related: chemistry is visible when characters reveal genuine inner life through the comedy, and heart is present when comedy serves the love story rather than undercutting it.
entry_conditions: Leads are paired but audience cannot perceive why they would be drawn to each other
Comic situations are funny but do not reveal character or deepen romantic stakes
exit_conditions: This node represents genre failure â€” there is no successful exit condition; the story must be revised at the level of character, casting, or structural design
typical_variants: Leads are conventionally matched but temperamentally inert together â€” technically appropriate but without spark
Comedy is situation-driven but character-agnostic â€” any two people could be in these scenes
Romantic feeling is stated in dialogue but never dramatized through behavior or choice
Comic tone is sustained at the expense of the emotional truth the romance requires to feel real
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode â€” its presence indicates the genre contract has been violated
signals_in_text: Audience cannot articulate what each character sees in the other
Comic scenes could be swapped between different characters without loss
Romantic declarations feel like genre obligation rather than character truth
The resolution produces no emotional release â€” audience acknowledges it intellectually but does not feel it

edges.label: Promise requires romance and humor as co-equal engines
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Every scene must serve both the love story and the comic arc â€” stakes are measured in both romantic and comedic currency simultaneously
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must be written so their behavior generates humor and reveals genuine romantic feeling in the same action
common_alternatives: Dramedy shifts the balance toward emotional weight
Pure comedy abandons the romantic arc when it becomes inconvenient
anti_patterns: Genre drifts into romance drama when emotional stakes rise
Humor is used to deflect every genuine romantic moment rather than coexist with it

edges.label: Promise requires mismatch or conflict before union
preconditions: Genre promise established
Central couple identified
edges.effects_on_stakes: Romantic union carries weight only because incompatibility was genuine and had to be overcome
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must change or reveal hidden compatibility through the comic arc â€” they cannot simply wait out the obstacles
common_alternatives: Literary romance may begin with compatibility and introduce external obstacles instead
anti_patterns: Leads are immediately and mutually attracted â€” obstacles are purely circumstantial and the comic friction never runs through their relationship

edges.label: Promise requires social friction as generator of comedy and obstacles
preconditions: Genre promise established
Social world around couple established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Comedy and obstacles arise from the social world, not purely from character psychology or arbitrary circumstance
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must navigate social expectations, judgments, and interference in addition to their own feelings
common_alternatives: Psychological romance locates all obstacles in internal character conflict without social dimension
anti_patterns: Social world is decorative â€” present in scenes but does not generate friction, complications, or comic situations

edges.label: Promise requires resolution delivering both romantic and comic satisfaction
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Audience anticipates a resolution that fulfills both the romantic contract (union or commitment) and the comic contract (the final obstacle is overcome with wit or charm)
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must arrive at union in a way that is consistent with their comic personalities â€” the resolution must feel like them
common_alternatives: Pure romance ends with unalloyed sincerity
Pure comedy may ironize the romantic ending
anti_patterns: Resolution abandons all comic energy for straight dramatic sincerity
Resolution ironizes the romantic union to the point where audience doesn't know if the couple is really together

edges.label: Promise requires tone of wit, warmth, and playful energy
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Tone signals that the audience is safe â€” even painful moments in the story will ultimately resolve in warmth rather than devastation
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' vulnerabilities are observed with affection rather than contempt or clinical detachment
common_alternatives: Dark romantic comedy may hold more ironic distance while still preserving warmth
Satirical rom-com may be sharper but must retain genuine feeling for the central couple
anti_patterns: Cold or cynical tone that regards the romantic arc with contempt
Sentimental tone that abandons wit entirely

edges.label: Promise is violated when chemistry is absent or comedy is heartless
preconditions: Leads are present but audience perceives no attraction
Comedy operates independently of romantic feeling
edges.effects_on_stakes: Without chemistry, romantic stakes collapse â€” audience has no investment in whether the couple unites
edges.effects_on_character: Without heart in the comedy, characters are comic functions rather than people â€” audience cannot root for them
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern description â€” it names what the promise forbids

edges.label: Mismatch expressed as verbal combat and wit
preconditions: Mismatch is primarily one of temperament, wit, and social position
Both leads are capable of verbal sparring
edges.effects_on_stakes: Romantic stakes are embedded in the verbal combat â€” winning the argument and winning the person are connected
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are defined by their speed, wit, and social agility â€” the comedy reveals who they really are under the performance
common_alternatives: Workplace rom-com
Friends-to-lovers
Fake relationship
anti_patterns: Sparring is one-sided or becomes genuinely hostile rather than charged

edges.label: Mismatch expressed through professional conflict
preconditions: Mismatch is rooted in professional hierarchy, competing goals, or incompatible work styles
Professional setting creates ongoing friction
edges.effects_on_stakes: Romantic stakes compete with professional stakes â€” choosing each other may cost something professionally
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must integrate their professional identity with their emerging romantic self
common_alternatives: Screwball
Friends-to-lovers
Fake relationship
anti_patterns: Workplace is merely a backdrop without professional stakes shaping the romance

edges.label: Mismatch as internal denial of obvious compatibility
preconditions: Leads are established friends who are well-matched but deny romantic potential
Comedy arises from dramatic irony â€” audience sees what characters cannot
edges.effects_on_stakes: Romantic stakes include the friendship itself â€” admission of love risks losing what already exists
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must overcome self-protective denial and risk the established relationship for romantic possibility
common_alternatives: Screwball
Workplace rom-com
Fake relationship
anti_patterns: Friendship is stated but not shown â€” no real warmth or history between leads

edges.label: Mismatch formalized in contractual opposition
preconditions: Leads enter an arrangement that formally defines them as romantic partners while privately treating each other as contractual partners
Mismatch between the performed relationship and the real one generates comedy
edges.effects_on_stakes: The arrangement creates a deadline or external pressure that forces the romantic question before characters are ready
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must perform feelings they deny having, and the performance gradually becomes real
common_alternatives: Screwball
Workplace rom-com
Friends-to-lovers
anti_patterns: Fake arrangement lacks a plausible external reason, making the premise feel arbitrary

edges.label: Screwball requires setting that ensures repeated sparring
preconditions: Screwball subgenre selected
Both leads are present in a shared world
edges.effects_on_stakes: Setting provides ongoing occasions for verbal combat â€” the arena for sparring must be sustainable
edges.effects_on_character: Characters have no escape from each other and must evolve their dynamic in the face of repeated encounter
common_alternatives: High society gatherings
Shared estate or vehicle
Competitive professional context
anti_patterns: Leads can easily avoid each other, deflating the sparring dynamic

edges.label: Workplace rom-com requires coworker social audience
preconditions: Workplace rom-com subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Coworkers witness and amplify the romantic arc â€” professional and social reputation are at stake alongside personal feeling
edges.effects_on_character: Leads must manage their public professional image while navigating private romantic feelings
common_alternatives: Close friend group
Family observers
Rival company employees
anti_patterns: Workplace exists but coworkers are inert â€” they observe without affecting the story

edges.label: Friends-to-lovers relies on gap between public friendship and private feeling
preconditions: Friends-to-lovers subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: The friendship is public; the romantic feeling is private â€” the gap between them generates sustained comic irony and dramatic pressure
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must maintain the friend performance while managing feelings that complicate it
common_alternatives: Professional context where friendship is also publicly defined
Family setting where both leads are known as friends
anti_patterns: Characters' private feelings are visible to everyone â€” no gap between public and private behavior to exploit

edges.label: Fake relationship requires social audience to perform for
preconditions: Fake relationship subgenre selected
A social audience must believe in the fake relationship
edges.effects_on_stakes: The fake relationship exists for the benefit of the social audience â€” without them, there is no reason for the performance and no comic stakes
edges.effects_on_character: Leads must calibrate their performance for the audience while managing real feelings that complicate it
common_alternatives: Family gathering requiring sustained performance
Professional context where the relationship has reputational value
Social circle where both leads are known
anti_patterns: Fake relationship is performed but no one cares whether it is real â€” social stakes are absent

edges.label: Fake relationship is built on public-private divergence
preconditions: Fake relationship subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: The entire comic premise is the gap between public performance (romantic partners) and private reality (contractual strangers or reluctant collaborators) â€” this gap must be sustained and mined
edges.effects_on_character: Each private moment threatens to collapse the public performance; each public performance infiltrates and reshapes the private reality
anti_patterns: Leads drop the pretense in private immediately and behave as a real couple long before the genre's arc requires it

edges.label: Setting generates the charged first encounter
preconditions: Setting established with its forcing mechanism
Neither lead has yet made a strong impression on the other
edges.effects_on_stakes: The first encounter establishes both the comic dynamic and the romantic possibility â€” it must happen in a context that makes subsequent forced contact feel inevitable
edges.effects_on_character: Characters form strong first impressions (usually wrong) that will take the whole story to revise
common_alternatives: Meet-cute may precede the forcing mechanism and retroactively explain why they are in the same world
First encounter may be unpleasant enough to motivate one character to try to escape the setting
anti_patterns: First encounter is a mundane introduction â€” no friction, no comedy, no romantic charge

edges.label: Social audience amplifies and escalates misunderstandings
preconditions: Social audience established and active
Central misunderstanding in motion
edges.effects_on_stakes: Misunderstandings don't stay between the two leads â€” they travel through the social network and generate secondary misreadings that compound the original
edges.effects_on_character: Characters receive distorted information about each other mediated through the social audience, making correction harder
common_alternatives: Best friend who gives wrong advice based on their own misreading
Rival who strategically amplifies the misunderstanding
Parent or authority figure who intervenes based on false information
anti_patterns: Misunderstanding exists only between the two leads â€” no social amplification â€” remains a two-person problem with an easy fix

edges.label: Gap between public and private behavior generates peak misunderstanding
preconditions: Public-private divergence established as a pattern
A moment where the private truth is partially visible in the wrong context
edges.effects_on_stakes: The misunderstanding peaks when a character's private behavior or feeling is witnessed out of context and misread as confirming the wrong impression
edges.effects_on_character: Characters cannot correct the misreading without revealing the private truth they are not yet ready to admit
common_alternatives: Overheard conversation that is misinterpreted
Physical closeness or gesture witnessed and misread
Written or digital communication seen by the wrong person
anti_patterns: Public-private gap exists but is never witnessed by the wrong person â€” no misunderstanding generated

edges.label: First impression established in meet-cute becomes the wrong assumption escalated
preconditions: Meet-cute has established a specific wrong impression or dynamic
Story has built on that wrong impression across subsequent scenes
edges.effects_on_stakes: The escalation scene reveals how much the wrong impression has shaped both characters' choices â€” the cost of the misunderstanding becomes clear
edges.effects_on_character: Both leads have been acting on a false understanding of each other that must now be confronted
common_alternatives: The first impression may be reversed â€” the escalation makes clear how wrong the initial read was
anti_patterns: Escalation scene has no connection to the specific wrong impression established in the meet-cute

edges.label: Misunderstanding clears and creates space for romantic gesture
preconditions: Misunderstanding has peaked and begun to clear, or is cleared in the same scene as the gesture
At least one character now has accurate information about the other's true feelings or situation
edges.effects_on_stakes: The grand gesture is only possible after the misunderstanding clears â€” it requires the character to act on truth rather than false impression
edges.effects_on_character: Character making the gesture must be willing to be vulnerable and possibly wrong â€” the gesture is a risk, not a certainty
common_alternatives: Gesture precipitates the clearing of misunderstanding rather than following it
Both leads make gestures simultaneously as they arrive at truth together
anti_patterns: Grand gesture is made while misunderstanding is still active â€” character declares love based on false information, which undercuts the romantic weight

edges.label: Dual payoff is delivered through the gesture scene
preconditions: Both romantic and comic arcs are active and awaiting resolution
edges.effects_on_stakes: The gesture scene must pay off both arcs simultaneously â€” it is not enough to declare love; the declaration must also be emotionally and tonally consistent with the story's comic personality
edges.effects_on_character: The character making the gesture reveals who they have become through the comic arc â€” they are wiser, more vulnerable, and still themselves
common_alternatives: Resolution may be split across two scenes â€” the comic resolution slightly precedes the romantic declaration
Both characters contribute to the gesture in ways that reflect their respective arcs
anti_patterns: Gesture is purely earnest with no comic personality
Gesture is purely comedic with no genuine romantic vulnerability

edges.label: Failure to balance comedy and romance produces chemistry deficiency
preconditions: One engine (romance or comedy) dominates to the exclusion of the other
edges.effects_on_stakes: When comedy dominates, the romantic stakes feel unreal; when romance dominates, the comedy feels forced
edges.effects_on_character: Characters become either comic functions (funny but not lovable) or romantic ideals (beautiful but not funny) rather than full people
anti_patterns: This edge names the failure state â€” it identifies what must not happen

edges.label: Loss of warmth or wit collapses into chemistry deficiency
preconditions: Tone has shifted to cold wit without warmth, or warm sentiment without wit
edges.effects_on_stakes: Cold wit produces comedy without heart â€” audience laughs but does not feel; warm sentiment without wit produces romance without chemistry â€” audience feels but is not charmed
edges.effects_on_character: Characters become either comedians or romantic leads rather than both simultaneously
anti_patterns: This edge names the tonal failure â€” either half of the tonal equation without the other
